text: During the total laparoscopic hysterectomy, the surgeon uses a ligature device to coagulate the right round ligament, located four centimeters lateral to the uterus. The fallopian tube is positioned three centimeters away, and the ovarian ligament, two centimeters away, is being coagulated to prevent reverse bleeding from the uterus side.

The surgeon positions the uterine manipulator towards the contralateral shoulder, ensuring the uterus is neither antiverted nor retroverted. The left side upper pedicles are addressed, starting with the round ligament approximately four centimeters lateral to the uterus, followed by the fallopian tube at three centimeters, and the ovarian ligament. The anterior leaf of the broad ligament is stretched with the left hand and cut with the right hand, moving the uterus to a retroverted position at five, six, and seven o'clock. The bladder is separated from the uterus, revealing a crisscross pattern of vessels, and the bladder pillars are separated on both sides to ensure the ureter moves laterally.

The surgeon positions the uterus in an antiverted orientation at a one o'clock position. Using a harmonic or monopolar device, the posterior peritoneum is stretched and cut above the arc of the uterosacral ligament. The uterine hump on the left side is visible during this process.

The uterus is positioned at the eleven o'clock position for cutting the right side of the posterior peritoneum. The surgeon stretches the peritoneum before cutting to avoid bleeding from the vein, which is posterior to the artery. The uterine hump on the right side is clearly visible. The surgeon then moves to the uterine pedicle, using a ligature to coagulate the ascending part before reaching the uterine artery, ensuring not to cut at this stage.

The surgeon coagulates the uterine artery on the right side, causing the uterus to appear paler as the blood supply is stopped. The uterine artery is then cut, and the cervicovaginal fascia is separated. The left uterine pedicle is cut, and the tip of the uterine is visible. The uterus is pushed towards the contralateral shoulder, and the right side is addressed with further coagulation and cutting, ensuring posterior structures are properly managed.

The surgeon secures the uterine artery, causing the uterus to appear pale, indicating cessation of blood supply. A colpotomizer of appropriate size (3.5 or 4 cm) is positioned, and the harmonic device is used to perform the colpotomy. The assistant applies pressure to the colpotomizer, facilitating the cutting process as the vibrating tip of the harmonic contacts the tissue.

The surgeon completes the anterior and left side of the colpotomy, then proceeds to the posterior part, carefully avoiding cutting the uterosacral ligament. The uterus is now free and is pulled through the vagina using the colpotomizer.

The surgeon performs a bilateral salpingectomy, applying anteromedial traction to the fallopian tubes to separate them from the ovaries. The infundibular ovarian ligament is grasped with a ligature to minimize collateral damage to the ovary and artery. The surgeon then begins suturing the vaginal vault, taking sequential bites through the uterine, ligamentous, and vaginal tissues, creating a double-layer closure.

The surgeon completes the final knot for the vault closure, ensuring the anterior and posterior vaginal fascia and epithelium are included to prevent granulation tissue formation. The intracorporeal suturing technique is used, terminating with a surgeon's knot. The suturing starts from the right uterosacral ligament, continues to the left, and returns to approximate the angles. Care is taken to hold the suture near the needle to avoid overshooting and potential injury to the iliac vessel or bowel.
